title: Bourbon Spain
subtitle: 1700–1808 · War of Succession, Enlightenment and Reform

categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: The Bourbon kings — dynastic rupture and enlightened reform

entries.label: Philip V — the War of Spanish Succession
entries.hint: Philip V, grandson of Louis XIV, inherits Spain from the last Habsburg — triggering the 13-year War of Spanish Succession that involves most of Europe; the Peace of Utrecht (1713) confirms him as king but strips Spain of the Netherlands, Sardinia, Naples and Gibraltar.
entries.note: Philip V's accession: Gaceta de Madrid, Nov 1700. Peace of Utrecht: AGS Estado; also in the British National Archives (SP 108/402). The loss of Gibraltar (Treaty of Utrecht Art.10, 1713): text in Davenport (1917). Philip V abdicated briefly 1724 (to his son Luis I who died after 7 months) — documented in Gaceta de Madrid. Kamen (2001), Philip V of Spain: the standard English biography.

entries.label: Marques de la Ensenada -- four secretariats, one minister
entries.hint: Zenon de Somodevilla, Marques de la Ensenada, holds the Secretariats of State, War, Navy and Finance simultaneously under Ferdinand VI -- he commissions the Catastro (cadastral survey) to reform taxation and rebuilds the navy; secret negotiations with France against Britain are discovered by the British ambassador Keene, leading to his dismissal in 1754 under direct British pressure.
entries.note: Catastro de Ensenada (1749-1756): AHN, extant. Naval rebuilding: AGS Marina. The 'Ensenada conspiracy' and his fall (Jul 1754): documented in British and Spanish diplomatic correspondence (Keene's despatches, UK National Archives SP 94). Rodriguez Casado (1962), La politica y los politicos en el reinado de Carlos III: contextualises Ensenada's legacy for the following reign.

entries.label: Ferdinand VI -- the pacifist interlude
entries.hint: Ferdinand VI keeps Spain neutral throughout nearly his entire reign -- the Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle (1748) ends his father's wars; under Ensenada, the navy is rebuilt from roughly 30 to 50 ships of the line and the Catastro cadastral survey (1749-1756) lays groundwork for fiscal reform; the Royal Academy of Fine Arts is founded (1752); his death without heirs in 1759 brings Charles III from Naples.
entries.note: Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle 1748: AHN Estado. Catastro de Ensenada (1749-1756): AHN -- extant, one of the most complete 18th-century cadastral surveys in Europe. Naval rebuilding figures: AGS Marina. Real Academia de Bellas Artes de San Fernando founding decree 1752: BNE. Ferdinand VI's death without heirs Aug 1759: documented -- Charles III recalled from Naples.

entries.label: Jose de Carvajal y Lancaster -- the pro-British counterweight
entries.hint: Jose de Carvajal y Lancaster serves as Secretary of State alongside Ensenada under Ferdinand VI -- the rivalry between pro-French Ensenada and pro-British Carvajal IS Ferdinand VI's neutrality policy, a deliberate balance of factions rather than a single coherent line; Carvajal negotiates the 1750 Treaty of Madrid abolishing the British asiento; his death in 1754 removes the counterweight and Ensenada falls the same year under British pressure -- the two ministers' careers begin and end together.
entries.note: Treaty of Madrid 1750 (Carvajal's negotiation): AHN Estado -- documented in entry ex7. Carvajal's death Apr 1754 and Ensenada's fall Jul 1754 (same year): documented in Keene's despatches (UK National Archives SP 94). Delgado Barrado (2001), Aquiles y Teresa: Carvajal y Ensenada: the standard study of the rivalry as the structural mechanism of Ferdinand VI's neutrality.

entries.label: Charles III — Spain's great enlightened monarch
entries.hint: Charles III is the paradigm of Bourbon enlightened despotism — expels the Jesuits (1767), centralises administration, promotes trade, builds roads and canals, reforms the colonies; his reforms transform Spain but create tensions that will explode in 1808.
entries.note: Jesuit expulsion decree: Pragmática Sanción, 27 Feb 1767, Gaceta de Madrid. The Bourbon administrative reforms (Nueva Planta decrees): AGS. Lynch (1989), Bourbon Spain 1700–1808: the standard English study. Charles III's building programme in Madrid: documented in Archivo de la Villa de Madrid. Herr (1958), The Eighteenth-Century Revolution in Spain: analyses the reform programme.

entries.label: Conde de Floridablanca -- the Bourbon reform programme's architect
entries.hint: Jose Monino, Count of Floridablanca, serves as First Secretary of State from 1777, presiding over the Junta de Estado (proto-cabinet, 1787) that coordinates the Bourbon reform programme -- the Sociedades Economicas de Amigos del Pais, road-building, colonial administration; his Instruccion Reservada (1787) is the primary policy blueprint; dismissed in 1792 amid fears following the French Revolution.
entries.note: Junta de Estado founding decree 1787: AHN. Floridablanca's Instruccion Reservada (1787): AHN -- the primary policy document, published in modern critical editions. Sociedades Economicas network: documented in the Instruccion. His dismissal Feb 1792: court correspondence, AHN Estado. Olaechea (1998), El conde de Floridablanca: the standard biography.

entries.label: Charles IV -- the king who could not choose
entries.hint: Charles IV, by most accounts a well-meaning but politically passive monarch, presides over the dynasty's final collapse -- dominated first by his wife Maria Luisa and then by her favourite Manuel Godoy; his reign opens with the secret repeal of the Salic Law (Cortes of Madrid, 1789) and closes with his forced abdication at Aranjuez (March 1808), days before Napoleon summons both Charles and his son Ferdinand to Bayonne and effectively annexes the throne.
entries.note: Charles IV's accession 1788: AHN. The Aranjuez abdication 19 Mar 1808: AHN Estado -- forced by the Aranjuez mutiny (entry k8). Bayonne abdications (May 1808): documented, the direct trigger for the Peninsular War covered in the next folio. La Parra Lopez (2018), Carlos IV: the standard modern biography reassessing his passive reputation.

entries.label: Manuel Godoy -- favourite, 'Principe de la Paz', and the Aranjuez mutiny
entries.hint: Manuel Godoy rises from royal bodyguard to First Secretary of State (1792) through Queen Maria Luisa's favour -- he negotiates the Peace of Basel (1795, earning the title Principe de la Paz), the disastrous San Ildefonso alliance (1796) and Fontainebleau (1807); his unpopularity culminates in the Escorial Conspiracy (Oct 1807, led by the heir Ferdinand) and the Aranjuez mutiny (17-19 Mar 1808), which forces Charles IV's abdication; Godoy survives but is exiled, dying in Paris in 1851.
entries.note: Godoy's appointment 1792: AHN Estado. Escorial Conspiracy Oct 1807: AHN -- documents Ferdinand's plot against his own father and Godoy. Aranjuez mutiny 17-19 Mar 1808: AHN Estado -- the popular uprising that storms Godoy's residence and forces the abdication. Godoy's own Memorias (1836, written in exile): the primary self-justifying account. La Parra Lopez (2002), Manuel Godoy: la aventura del poder: the standard modern biography.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: War of Succession, colonial wars and the road to Trafalgar

entries.label: Battle of Almansa — Bourbon victory in Spain
entries.hint: The Duke of Berwick defeats the Austro-British allied army at Almansa — securing Castile and Aragon for Philip V; the decisive battle of the War of Spanish Succession in Spain, fought by a French marshal commanding a Spanish army against a British-led force.
entries.note: Military records: AGS Guerra Moderna. The battle's outcome: confirmed across French, British and Spanish sources. The Decreto de Nueva Planta for Valencia (29 Jun 1707): issued directly after Almansa — Gaceta de Madrid. The paradox: a French marshal won Castile for the Spanish crown. Kamen (1969), The War of Succession in Spain 1700–1715: the standard study.

entries.label: Peace of Utrecht — Spain's European losses
entries.hint: The Treaty of Utrecht (1713-1715) ends the War of Spanish Succession -- Spain cedes Gibraltar and Menorca to Britain, the Spanish Netherlands and Italian possessions to Austria, and grants Britain the asiento (slave-trading monopoly) and navio de permiso (annual trade ship) -- the commercial concessions that directly cause the War of Jenkins' Ear three decades later; Philip V retains the Spanish throne but the treaty marks Spain's definitive demotion from the first rank of European powers.
entries.note: Treaty of Utrecht 1713 (and supplementary 1715 treaties): AHN Estado, British National Archives SP 94. The asiento clause: directly cited in the War of Jenkins' Ear entry (c4) as the root commercial dispute. Kamen (2001), Philip V of Spain: The King Who Reigned Twice, ch.4: the standard account of the territorial losses. Gibraltar and Menorca cessions: Article X and XI of the treaty -- the territorial clauses still cited in modern Gibraltar sovereignty disputes.

entries.label: War of Jenkins' Ear -- Blas de Lezo's defence of Cartagena
entries.hint: The Anglo-Spanish War nominally triggered by Captain Jenkins's severed ear (1731) is rooted in disputes over the asiento slave-trading contract and Caribbean smuggling; the Battle of Cartagena de Indias (Mar-May 1741) is Spain's greatest colonial military victory -- Blas de Lezo, with a fraction of the numbers, repels a British assault force of 186 ships and 27,000 men; the war merges into the wider War of the Austrian Succession and ends with the Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle (1748).
entries.note: Battle of Cartagena de Indias (Mar-May 1741): AGS Guerra -- Blas de Lezo's dispatches. British fleet records (Admiral Vernon): UK National Archives ADM series. The disproportion of forces: documented in both Spanish and British accounts -- one of the most one-sided naval defeats in British history. Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle 1748: AHN Estado. The asiento dispute originates in the Treaty of Utrecht (1713) -- direct connection to entry w1.

entries.label: War of the Austrian Succession -- the Italian theatre (1740-1748)
entries.hint: Driven by Elisabeth Farnese (Philip V's second wife), Spanish armies fight a gruelling campaign in Italy to secure the Duchies of Parma, Piacenza and Guastalla for her younger son Don Felipe -- a separate theatre of the same broader war as the Caribbean's Jenkins' Ear; the campaign exhausts Spain's treasury through the 1740s and directly dictates Ferdinand VI's subsequent commitment to absolute neutrality after 1748.
entries.note: Spanish military operations in Italy 1742-1748: AGS Guerra. Elisabeth Farnese's dynastic motivations: documented in her correspondence (AHN Estado) -- securing an Italian throne for Don Felipe (later Duke of Parma). Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle 1748 confirms Don Felipe's Parma duchy: AHN Estado. The fiscal exhaustion's direct connection to Ferdinand VI's neutrality (entry k4): documented in Ensenada's own fiscal reports.

entries.label: Motín de Esquilache — the riots that triggered the Jesuit expulsion
entries.hint: In March 1766 Madrid erupts in riots against Charles III's Italian minister Esquilache — ostensibly over a ban on long cloaks and broad-brimmed hats, but driven by bread price inflation; the king flees the capital; the Crown blames the Jesuits for instigating the riots, using this as the formal pretext for their expulsion in February 1767.
entries.note: The Motín de Esquilache March 1766: AGS and contemporary press. Charles III's flight to Aranjuez: documented. The secret investigation blaming Jesuits: AGS Gracia y Justicia. Lynch (1989), Bourbon Spain pp.254–261: the standard analysis of the Esquilache-expulsion connection. The Pragmática Sanción (1767) gives no stated reason — the Esquilache connection is documented only in the secret AGS report.

entries.label: Great Siege of Gibraltar (1779-1783) -- the longest siege in British history
entries.hint: As part of Spain's intervention in the American Revolutionary War, a combined Spanish-French force besieges Gibraltar for three years and seven months -- the longest siege endured by British forces in history; the 'floating batteries' assault of September 1782 fails catastrophically; the siege ends with the Treaty of Paris (1783) leaving Gibraltar British, but ties down enormous resources and is the most visible Spanish military effort of the war alongside Galvez's Gulf Coast campaigns.
entries.note: Siege duration (24 Jun 1779-7 Feb 1783, 1,316 days): AGS Guerra and British garrison records (TNA WO 284). The floating batteries assault (13 Sep 1782): documented in both Spanish and British accounts -- a major tactical failure. Treaty of Paris 1783: confirms British Gibraltar. Drinkwater (1786), A History of the Siege of Gibraltar: the standard contemporary British account. Falkner (2009), The Great Siege of Gibraltar 1779-1783: the standard modern study.

entries.label: War of the Pyrenees -- Spain joins the First Coalition (1793-1795)
entries.hint: Spain joins the First Coalition against Revolutionary France after Louis XVI's execution (Jan 1793) -- the War of the Pyrenees (Guerra de la Convencion) is fought with mixed results along the frontier; the Peace of Basel (22 Jul 1795) ends hostilities and earns Godoy the title 'Principe de la Paz'. It was the subsequent Second Treaty of San Ildefonso (1796) that formally committed Spain to an offensive-defensive alliance with France -- the realignment that leads directly to Trafalgar a decade later.
entries.note: Peace of Basel 22 Jul 1795: AHN Estado -- ends hostilities only. Second Treaty of San Ildefonso (1796, documented in exchange entry ex4): the actual offensive-defensive alliance commitment. Godoy's title 'Principe de la Paz': granted by Charles IV, documented in royal decree. Military operations: AGS Guerra. The realignment leading to the Treaty of San Ildefonso (1796) and ultimately Trafalgar (1805): the documented causal chain across this folio's exchange and conflict entries.

entries.label: War of the Oranges -- 18 days against Portugal (1801)
entries.hint: At Napoleon's insistence, Spain invades Portugal in May 1801 -- an 18-day campaign named the 'War of the Oranges' after Godoy sent orange blossoms from Elvas to Queen Maria Luisa; the Treaty of Badajoz (6 Jun 1801) ends it with Spain annexing Olivenza, a small but permanently disputed territory still claimed by Portugal today; the war demonstrates Spain's complete subordination to French strategic interests.
entries.note: Treaty of Badajoz 6 Jun 1801: AHN Estado. The Olivenza dispute remains formally unresolved -- documented in modern Spanish-Portuguese diplomatic correspondence, a rare case of an 1801 territorial question still live. Godoy's orange blossom anecdote: his own Memorias (1836). The 18-day campaign: AGS Guerra.

entries.label: Battle of Trafalgar — end of Spanish naval power
entries.hint: Nelson defeats the combined Franco-Spanish fleet at Trafalgar — 18 Spanish ships lost, 2,500 Spanish dead; Spain's naval power is broken permanently, making it impossible to maintain the American empire; Napoleon's invasion follows three years later.
entries.note: The battle: documented in both British National Archives (ADM 1/404, Nelson's log) and AGS Marina (Spanish fleet records). Spanish casualties: AGS Marina. The Spanish flagship Santísima Trinidad (112 guns, largest warship in the world): its last log entry is in AGS. Adkins (2004), Trafalgar: uses both British and Spanish archives. Spain's fleet never recovered: documented in AGS Marina statistics.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: Bourbon centralisation, the Nueva Planta and colonial reform

entries.label: Decreto de Nueva Planta — abolishing Catalan and Aragonese law
entries.hint: Following the War of Succession, Philip V abolishes the traditional laws and institutions of Aragon, Valencia, Mallorca and Catalonia — replacing them with Castilian law; this is the origin of the Catalan grievance that has never been fully resolved.
entries.note: Decreto de Nueva Planta for Catalonia (16 Jan 1716): Gaceta de Madrid, 18 Jan 1716 — text published. The Valencia decree (29 Jun 1707): Gaceta de Madrid. The Catalan institutions abolished: Generalitat, Consell de Cent, local law codes. Elliott (2018), Scots and Catalans: places the suppression in European comparative context. The 1716 decree is explicitly referenced in current Catalan independence debates.

entries.label: Bourbon colonial reforms — the intendant system
entries.hint: Charles III replaces the old Habsburg colonial councils with a system of intendants (royal governors with wide powers) across Spanish America — the most systematic reorganisation of the colonial administration since the 16th century; it increases revenue but creates resentment.
entries.note: Ordinance of Intendants for New Spain 1786: AGI, Mexico. The colonial fiscal data showing revenue increase: TePaske (2010). Lynch (1958), Spanish Colonial Administration 1782–1810: the standard study. The resentment the reforms created: documented in subsequent independence movements' grievance lists (e.g. Miranda's writings). The reform's unintended consequence — creating a creole administrative class that then leads independence — is a major historiographical theme.

entries.label: Municipal Reforms of 1766 -- Diputados del Comun and Sindico Personero
entries.hint: Following the Esquilache riots (entry c3), Campomanes drafts decrees creating the elected offices of Diputados del Comun and Sindico Personero -- forcing the closed oligarchic town councils (regidores, who held hereditary or purchased seats) across Spain to accept popularly elected representatives overseeing grain supply and markets; the most significant reform of local municipal government in the 18th century, though implementation varies enormously by region.
entries.note: Royal decree creating Diputados del Comun and Sindico Personero 1766: AHN, Consejos. Campomanes's role: documented in his own Discurso sobre el fomento de la industria popular (1774). Guerrero Mayllo (1993), Familia y vida cotidiana de una elite de poder: contextualises the regidores' closed oligarchy that the reform challenges. The connection to the Esquilache riots (c3): explicitly causal -- documented in the preamble of the 1766 decree itself.

entries.label: Cortes of Madrid (1789) -- the secret repeal of the Salic Law
entries.hint: On his accession, Charles IV convokes the Cortes of Madrid to swear allegiance to his heir -- but the delegates secretly vote to revoke the Salic Law that Philip V had introduced in 1713 barring women from inheriting the crown; Charles IV keeps the decision secret and unpublished, but this hidden act lays the direct legal foundation for the succession crisis that becomes the Carlist Wars in the 19th century, when Ferdinand VII's daughter Isabel II's right to inherit is contested using exactly this unresolved 1789 reversal.
entries.note: The 1789 Cortes proceedings and secret vote: AHN, Consejos -- the pragmatica sancion was signed but never published (pragmatica that 'remained in the king's breast', in the contemporary phrase). Philip V's 1713 Salic Law: AHN -- the original exclusion this reverses. The direct connection to the Carlist Wars (1833 onwards, documented in the 1808-1900 folio): Burdiel (2010), Isabel II: una biografia, ch.1 -- the 1789 secret repeal is the legal basis Ferdinand VII later invokes (1830 Pragmatic Sanction publication) for Isabel's succession, which the Carlists reject.

entries.label: Novisima Recopilacion (1805) -- the last Bourbon legal codification
entries.hint: The Novisima Recopilacion de las Leyes de Espana (1805) is the last great compilation of royal decrees before the 19th-century codification movement -- Spain in 1805 is an absolute monarchy with no constitution, and the Recopilacion is a tool of administrative absolutism reinforcing the sovereign will, organising centuries of decrees and Bourbon reforms into a single reference; its publication under Charles IV, three years before the dynasty's collapse, is both the culmination of Bourbon administrative rationalism and an unintentional monument to the system's imminent obsolescence.
entries.note: Novisima Recopilacion, Royal Cedula 15 Jul 1805: BNE -- primary, multiple extant printed editions. Tomas y Valiente (1983), Manual de Historia del Derecho Espanol: the standard legal history. The work's near-immediate obsolescence (Spain's first liberal Constitution follows in 1812): documented as a textbook case of codification arriving too late.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: National Catholicism under pressure — Enlightenment vs the Church

entries.label: The Inquisition under Bourbon reform — declining but present
entries.hint: The Bourbon Inquisition is significantly less active than its Habsburg predecessor — the last person burned alive at the stake by the Spanish Inquisition is María de los Dolores López (Seville, 1781); the 1826 execution of Cayetano Ripoll (hanging, by a diocesan board, not the Inquisition itself) is outside this folio's 1808 boundary; Goya witnesses the Inquisition's proceedings and documents them.
entries.note: AHN Inquisición: the archive continues into the Bourbon period. The Índice de libros prohibidos: published editions 1707, 1747, 1790 — BNE. The last actual burning at the stake by the Inquisition: María de los Dolores López, Seville, 1781 — convicted of heretical quietism. The 1826 Cayetano Ripoll execution (hanging, by a diocesan 'Junta de Fe', not the official Inquisition) falls outside this folio's 1808 boundary. Goya's Inquisition scenes: Prado (painted c.1812–1819). Kamen (1997), The Spanish Inquisition: covers the Bourbon period.

entries.label: Concordat of 1753 -- regalism's triumph over Rome
entries.hint: The Concordat of 1753 transfers the right of ecclesiastical patronage -- the power to appoint to roughly 12,000 benefices across Spain -- from Rome to the Spanish Crown; it is the single greatest expansion of royal authority over the Church before 1851, the legal triumph of regalism, and the precondition for the 1767 Jesuit expulsion and the Bourbon reform programme's ability to direct Church wealth and personnel.
entries.note: Concordat 11 Jan 1753: AHN, Consejos -- primary. Negotiated by ambassador Manuel Ventura de Figueroa. Egido (1979), El regalismo y las relaciones Iglesia-Estado en el siglo XVIII: the standard study. The patronage transfer covering ~12,000 benefices: AHN. The explicit connection to the 1767 Jesuit expulsion (the Concordat removed the main legal obstacle): documented by historians of the expulsion.

entries.label: Jansenist-Ultramontane conflict -- the defining theological battle of Bourbon Spain
entries.loc: Salamanca; Valencia; Madrid (university faculties and royal councils)
entries.hint: Spanish Jansenists -- favouring regalism, simplified liturgy, vernacular scripture and the moral rigour associated with Port-Royal -- clash with Ultramontanes who defend papal supremacy and Jesuit theological models; the conflict splits clergy, university faculties (Salamanca, Valencia) and royal councils for decades; it directly drives the crown's campaign to subordinate the Church (the Concordat of 1753, the Jesuit expulsion of 1767) and provides the theological vocabulary for the broader regalist reform programme.
entries.note: Spanish Jansenism: Herr (1958), The Eighteenth-Century Revolution in Spain, ch.5: the standard English study. University faculty disputes (Salamanca, Valencia): documented in university archive records (AHN, Universidades). The connection to the Concordat of 1753 (r2) and Jesuit expulsion (r3): both explicitly framed by contemporary Jansenist polemicists as theological victories -- documented in the polemical literature (BNE). Mestre Sanchis (1976), Despotismo e Ilustracion en Espana: contextualises the Jansenist-regalist alliance.

entries.label: Expulsion of the Jesuits — 1767
entries.hint: Charles III expels the Society of Jesus from Spain and its colonies with 24 hours' notice — 2,641 Spanish Jesuits deported; the reason is never officially stated; the expulsion closes Jesuit schools and missions across the empire.
entries.note: Pragmática Sanción 27 Feb 1767: Gaceta de Madrid 2 Mar 1767 — text published. The sealed orders to colonial governors: AGI and AGS. The number deported: 2,641 from Spain, more from the colonies — documented in Jesuit records. Batllori (1966), La Cultura Hispano-Italiana de los Jesuitas Expulsos: documents the exiles' intellectual activity. The expulsion's connection to the Enlightenment critique of corporate power: Herr (1958).

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: Goya, neoclassicism and the Enlightenment aesthetic

entries.label: Churrigueresque — the exuberant Spanish Baroque
entries.hint: Churrigueresque -- characterised by the estipite (inverted-obelisk column) pioneered by the Churriguera family, seen at its purest in the Salamanca altarpieces -- represents the most exuberant phase of Spanish Baroque; Narciso Tome's Transparente of Toledo Cathedral (1732), often grouped with it, is more precisely a distinct Hispano-Rococo synthesis of Roman and French influences -- a light-infused theatrical stage-set rather than estipite Churrigueresque proper.
entries.note: San Esteban altarpiece, Salamanca (José Benito de Churriguera, 1692–1696): extant. The Transparente, Toledo Cathedral (Narciso Tomé, 1732): extant — the hidden cupola and its theatrical light are documented in cathedral building accounts (Archivo de la Catedral de Toledo). The Plaza Mayor of Salamanca (Alberto de Churriguera, 1729–1756): extant. Bonet Correa (1993), Figuras, modelos e imágenes: the standard study. The Churrigueresque's relationship to Neoclassicism: framed as excess to be corrected — documented in Ventura Rodríguez's own writings.
keywords: Narciso Tome Transparente of Toledo Cathedral

entries.label: Ventura Rodríguez and Bourbon neoclassicism
entries.hint: Ventura Rodríguez is the leading architect of Spanish neoclassicism — his work for Charles III transforms Madrid; the Pilar basilica in Zaragoza (completed 1765) and the Pamplona Cathedral façade (1783) are the primary surviving monuments.
entries.note: Ventura Rodríguez's building records: Archivo de la Villa de Madrid. The Pilar basilica, Zaragoza: extant. The Pamplona Cathedral façade: extant. Reese (1976), The Architecture of Ventura Rodríguez: the standard study. Charles III's urban reform of Madrid (the Paseo del Prado planted 1775): documented in Archivo de la Villa de Madrid.

entries.label: Moratin and neoclassical theatre reform -- El si de las ninas (1806)
entries.hint: Nicolas Fernandez de Moratin and his son Leandro lead the neoclassical reform of Spanish drama -- campaigning to ban traditional auto sacramentales and Baroque theatrical excess in favour of structured, secular plays designed to educate audiences in Enlightenment civic virtues; Leandro's El si de las ninas (1806), a comedy attacking arranged marriages between young women and old men, is the movement's masterpiece and the last major literary work of the Bourbon Enlightenment before 1808.
entries.note: El si de las ninas (1806): first performed Teatro de la Cruz, Madrid -- BNE holds the original printed edition. Nicolas Fernandez de Moratin's Desengano al teatro espanol (1762-63): the founding polemic against auto sacramentales -- BNE. The royal ban on autos sacramentales (1765, Aranda's administration): AHN -- the direct policy outcome of the Moratin circle's campaign. Andioc (1976), Teatro y sociedad en el Madrid del siglo XVIII: the standard study of the reform's social context.

entries.label: Luigi Boccherini in Madrid — Spanish-Italian musical fusion
entries.hint: The Italian cellist-composer Luigi Boccherini spends 36 years in Madrid — his 'Spanish' style (incorporating fandango, seguidilla rhythms) creates a unique fusion; his chamber music is performed throughout Europe from Madrid manuscripts.
entries.note: Boccherini's appointment to Infante Luis's court 1770: Archivo del Palacio Real. His Madrid manuscripts: Biblioteca Nacional de España holds the primary collection. Gérard (1969), Catalogue of the Works of Luigi Boccherini: the standard catalogue. Boccherini's G major Guitar Quintet (Op. 30 No. 6) with fandango finale: the most widely performed example of the Spanish-Italian fusion.

entries.label: Goya — from court painter to witness of disaster
entries.hint: Goya is appointed court painter in 1786 and paints the royal family with merciless psychological honesty — his portraits of Charles IV's family (1800–1801) are the most penetrating royal portraits of the 18th century; he simultaneously documents the darkness beneath the Enlightenment surface.
entries.note: Goya's court appointment 25 Jun 1786: Archivo del Palacio Real. The Family of Charles IV (1800–1801): Prado (P000726). Los Caprichos (1799): published by Goya himself, sold in his shop — BNE holds copies. The Disasters of War etchings (made c.1810–1820): Prado. Goya's complaint to the Inquisition: AHN Inquisición (he was investigated for Los Caprichos). Hughes (2003), Goya: the standard modern biography.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Spanish Enlightenment — Feijóo, Jovellanos and the reforming intellect

entries.label: Spanish Enlightenment and the Real Academia Española
entries.hint: Philip V founds the Real Academia Española in 1713 — modelled on the Académie française, its purpose is to 'purify' and standardise the Spanish language; its first dictionary (1726–1739) and grammar (1771) are the foundational documents of modern standard Spanish.
entries.note: Royal warrant founding the RAE: 3 Oct 1714, Gaceta de Madrid. Diccionario de Autoridades (1726–1739, 6 vols): BNE — the first comprehensive Spanish dictionary with literary citations. The 1771 Gramática: BNE. Lázaro Carreter (1949), Las ideas lingüísticas en España durante el siglo XVIII: the standard study. The RAE's founding documents: Archivo de la Real Academia Española, Madrid.

entries.label: Melchor de Macanaz -- the founder of Spanish regalist thought
entries.hint: Melchor de Macanaz, Philip V's fiscal agent during the War of Succession, writes radical legal memorials proposing the absolute subordination of Church courts to royal jurisdiction, taxation of clergy, and restriction of the Inquisition's independence; condemned by the Inquisition itself and exiled to France for over three decades, his thought nonetheless lays the direct legal and philosophical foundation for the entire 18th-century regalist programme -- the Concordat of 1753, the Jesuit expulsion, and the broader subordination of Church to Crown.
entries.note: Macanaz's memorials (1713-1714, especially the Pedimento): AHN, Consejos -- primary, the Pedimento fiscal is the foundational regalist text. His Inquisition condemnation and French exile (1715-1748): documented in Inquisition trial records (AHN, Inquisicion). Olaechea (1965), Las relaciones hispano-romanas en la segunda mitad del XVIII: traces Macanaz's direct intellectual influence on Floridablanca and Campomanes two generations later. His eventual rehabilitation (1760, under Charles III): documented -- vindication of regalist thought as state orthodoxy.

entries.label: Feijóo — Teatro Crítico Universal
entries.hint: Benito Feijóo's Teatro Crítico Universal (8 volumes, 1726–1739) attacks superstition and promotes scientific inquiry — it is the most widely read work of the Spanish Enlightenment, going through 14 editions in the 18th century; the Royal Council of Castile prohibits criticism of it.
entries.note: First volume 1726 (Madrid): BNE. The Royal Council prohibition on criticism (1750): Gaceta de Madrid. The 14 editions by 1800: documented in publication records. Herr (1958), The Eighteenth-Century Revolution in Spain, ch.2. Feijóo's influence on the subsequent generation of reformers: documented in their own writings.

entries.label: Conde de Campomanes -- the theorist of agrarian and municipal reform
entries.loc: Madrid (Royal Council of Castile; Real Sociedad Economica)
entries.hint: Pedro Rodriguez de Campomanes serves as fiscal of the Council of Castile and is the chief legal theorist behind both the Jesuit expulsion (which he helped engineer) and the Municipal Reforms of 1766; his Discurso sobre el fomento de la industria popular (1774) and Discurso sobre la educacion popular de los artesanos (1775) argue that guild restrictions and clerical landholding strangle the economy -- the intellectual blueprint for Bourbon agrarian and fiscal reform that Jovellanos later builds on.
entries.note: Discurso sobre el fomento de la industria popular (1774) and Discurso sobre la educacion popular de los artesanos (1775): BNE -- primary, both went through multiple editions and were distributed to every Sociedad Economica de Amigos del Pais. Campomanes's role in the Jesuit expulsion: documented in his own Dictamen fiscal de expulsion de los jesuitas (1766-67, published 1977 from AHN manuscripts). Llombart (1992), Campomanes, economista y politico de Carlos III: the standard modern study. The direct line to Jovellanos's Informe Agrario (entry t2, 1794): explicitly acknowledged by Jovellanos.

entries.label: Cadalso -- Cartas marruecas and Enlightenment self-criticism
entries.hint: Jose Cadalso's Cartas marruecas (written 1774, published 1789 after his death) uses the device of a Moroccan traveller's letters to deliver one of the sharpest Enlightenment critiques of Spanish society -- attacking aristocratic idleness, educational neglect and national self-deception; modelled on Montesquieu's Persian Letters but distinctly Spanish in its melancholic tone, it is read as both an Enlightenment text and a precursor of Romantic disillusionment.
entries.note: Cartas marruecas: written 1774, first published in the Correo de Madrid 1789 (BNE) -- Cadalso died 1782, before publication. Cadalso's Noches lugubres (1789-1790): the companion text, an early Romantic meditation on grief. Glendinning (1962), Vida y obra de Cadalso: the standard biography. The Montesquieu model (Lettres persanes, 1721): explicitly acknowledged by Cadalso in the text's framing device.

entries.label: Archivo General de Indias — the empire's colonial memory
entries.hint: Charles III orders all colonial documents consolidated in the Casa Lonja in Seville — 43,000 bundles covering the administration of the Americas and Philippines from 1492 to independence; the most complete colonial administrative archive in the world.
entries.note: UNESCO Memory of the World (1997). Founded by royal decree 1785: AGI itself. Holdings: 43,000 legajos, 80 million pages, 8,000 maps. PARES digital access. The archive contains: Cortés's letters, Pizarro's orders, the Laws of Burgos, Las Casas's petitions, every colonial governor's reports — the most complete record of a colonial empire's administration in existence.

entries.label: Jovellanos — Informe sobre la Ley Agraria
entries.hint: Gaspar Melchor de Jovellanos's Report on the Agrarian Law is the most important Enlightenment economic document in Spanish history — arguing for the abolition of entail, common lands and guild restrictions; it influences Spanish liberalism for a century.
entries.note: First published 1795 (Madrid): BNE. The manuscript submitted to the Council of Castile 1794: AHN Consejos. Jovellanos's subsequent imprisonment by Godoy (1801–1808): documented in AGS. Caso González (1998), Jovellanos: the standard biography. The Informe's influence on the Cádiz Constitution debates (1810–1812): explicit references in the Diario de Sesiones.

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: Colonial trade reform and the Bourbon economic programme

entries.label: Reales Fábricas — Bourbon state manufactures
entries.loc: La Granja (glass); El Buen Retiro (porcelain); Santa Bárbara (tapestries); Seville (tobacco)
entries.hint: The Bourbon royal manufactories are the centrepiece of mercantilist economic theory in 18th-century Spain — La Granja produces glass and crystal, El Buen Retiro produces porcelain to rival Sèvres and Meissen, Santa Bárbara produces tapestries from Goya's cartoons, the Seville tobacco factory (1728) becomes the largest industrial building in Spain.
entries.note: La Granja Real Fábrica de Cristales: royal foundation documents (AGS). El Buen Retiro porcelain manufactory (founded 1760): Archivo del Palacio Real. Santa Bárbara tapestry factory: Goya's cartoons held in the Prado — 63 cartoons documented. The Seville Fábrica de Tabacos (1728, completed 1757): extant (now University of Seville). Lynch (1989), Bourbon Spain ch.5: the standard survey.

entries.label: Canal de Castilla -- linking the Castilian breadbasket to the sea
entries.hint: Begun under Ferdinand VI (1753) and continued under Charles III and IV, the Canal de Castilla is the largest internal infrastructure project of 18th-century Spain -- a canal network designed to link the wheat-producing plains of Old Castile to the northern ports, overcoming Spain's notoriously rugged internal terrain to build an integrated national grain market; never fully completed as planned, it nonetheless represents the most ambitious Bourbon attempt at internal economic integration.
entries.note: Canal de Castilla works records: AHN, Obras Publicas. The project's origins under Ferdinand VI 1753: documented in royal decree (AHN). Extant sections remain navigable today (tourist use) -- physical evidence. Madrazo (1984), El sistema de transportes en Espana 1750-1850: the standard study of why internal transport integration largely failed despite projects like this. The connection to the Esquilache riots' grain supply concerns (c3): both responses to the same structural problem of internal grain distribution.

entries.label: Banco de San Carlos (1782) — Spain's first national bank
entries.hint: Carlos III founds the Banco de San Carlos on 2 June 1782 — Spain's first national bank, conceived by the Count of Cabarrús to manage the soaring public debt and issue the vales reales (Spain's first paper currency) to fund wars against Britain; it is the direct institutional ancestor of the Bank of Spain.
entries.note: Founding royal decree 2 Jun 1782: Gaceta de Madrid. Cabarrús's proposal to Floridablanca (1780): the founding document — AHN Consejos. The vales reales (first Spanish paper currency): issued 1780 onwards — documented. Hamilton (1944), 'The Foundation of the Bank of Spain', Journal of Political Economy: the standard economic study. Tedde de Lorca (1988), El Banco de San Carlos 1782–1829: the standard modern study. The bank becomes the Banco de España in 1856 — the institutional lineage documented.

entries.label: Jorge Juan -- the 'English System' and the Santisima Trinidad
entries.hint: Jorge Juan y Santacilia, mathematician and naval officer, spies on British shipyards and subsequently establishes the 'Sistema del Jorge Juan' (English System) of naval architecture in Spain's own yards -- introducing advanced structural mathematics that produces some of the fastest and sturdiest ships of the line in the world, including the Santisima Trinidad (launched 1769), at the time the largest warship afloat; this is the technological engine behind Ensenada's naval buildup (entry k5).
entries.note: Jorge Juan's espionage mission to England (1749-1750, under the cover name 'Josephus Facio'): documented in his own reports (Museo Naval, Madrid). The Santisima Trinidad (launched Havana 1769, 130/140 guns): Museo Naval Madrid holds construction plans -- at launch the largest warship in the world. Jorge Juan, Examen maritimo theorico practico (1771): BNE -- the published treatise codifying the new shipbuilding mathematics. O'Donnell (2022), Jorge Juan: el espia que cambio la marina espanola: the standard modern study.
keywords: Sistema del Jorge Juan English System

entries.label: Royal Botanical Garden and the Malaspina Expedition -- Bourbon science
entries.loc: Madrid (Real Jardin Botanico); Pacific and Atlantic (Malaspina's voyage)
entries.hint: The Real Jardin Botanico de Madrid (relocated to its current site 1781) becomes the hub of Bourbon scientific expeditions -- the Malaspina Expedition (1789-1794) circumnavigates the empire with two purpose-built corvettes, collecting botanical, zoological and ethnographic data; alongside the Royal Botanical Expedition to New Granada (Mutis, from 1783) it represents the largest scientific data-gathering enterprise of the Spanish Enlightenment -- though much of it remains unpublished for over a century after Malaspina's political disgrace.
entries.note: Real Jardin Botanico founding/relocation 1781: CSIC institutional archive -- extant, now CSIC-administered. Malaspina expedition records: Museo Naval Madrid and Real Jardin Botanico archives -- over 70 volumes of manuscripts, many unpublished until the 20th century. Malaspina's arrest 1795 (for criticising Godoy) and suppression of findings: AHN. Pimentel (1991), La fisica de la monarquia: the standard study of Bourbon scientific policy.

entries.label: Real Observatorio Astronomico de Madrid (1790)
entries.hint: The Real Observatorio Astronomico de Madrid, founded by royal decree in 1790 and designed by Juan de Villanueva, completes the cluster of Bourbon scientific institutions alongside the Botanical Garden and the Natural History cabinet -- intended to support navigation, cartography and the verification of the new metric standards then being developed in France; its neoclassical building survives and remains in scientific use.
entries.note: Founding decree 1790: AHN. Villanueva's design: extant building, Real Observatorio de Madrid (now part of the Instituto Geografico Nacional) -- a rare case of an 18th-century scientific building still in continuous use. The connection to the French metric survey (documented in the France 1789-1815 folio, entry ex3): the Madrid observatory participated in the meridian verification network. Lafuente & Sellés (1988), El observatorio de Cadiz (1753-1831): contextualises the Madrid observatory within Spain's broader 18th-century astronomical infrastructure (Cadiz's observatory, founded 1753, predates Madrid's).

entries.label: Balmis Vaccine Expedition -- the first global vaccination campaign (1803)
entries.loc: Departs A Coruna; the Americas and Philippines
entries.hint: The Royal Philanthropic Vaccine Expedition, led by Francisco Javier de Balmis, departs A Coruna on 30 November 1803 carrying the smallpox vaccine to Spanish America and the Philippines -- using a relay of 22 orphan boys as living vaccine carriers, since the vaccine could not otherwise survive the voyage; it is the first mass international vaccination campaign in history, reaching hundreds of thousands of people across three continents.
entries.note: Expedition records: AHN, Estado. Balmis's own published account: Tratado historico y practico de la vacuna (1803) -- BNE. The 22 orphan boys ('vaccine chain'): documented -- several were adopted in Mexico and never returned, itself a Lives-adjacent story. Franco-Paredes et al. (2005), 'The Spanish Royal Philanthropic Expedition...', Clinical Infectious Diseases: the standard modern medical-historical study, cited by WHO as a precedent for global vaccination campaigns.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: Dynastic diplomacy, the Family Compact and the Atlantic world

entries.label: Manila Galleon -- the Pacific bridge between Asia and the empire
entries.hint: The Manila Galleon -- an annual voyage connecting the Philippines to Mexico, in continuous operation since 1565 -- remains active throughout the Bourbon period, carrying Chinese silk, porcelain and spices to the Americas in exchange for Mexican silver; it is the only direct trade link between Asia and the Americas before the 19th century, and the silver it carries circulates as far as China, where it underpins the Qing monetary system -- making this single shipping route a genuinely global economic artery.
entries.note: The Manila Galleon trade: Archivo General de Indias, Filipinas series. Schurz (1939), The Manila Galleon: the standard English study of the route's mechanics and cargo. The silver flow to China: documented in Flynn & Giraldez (1995), 'Born with a Silver Spoon', Journal of World History -- the global silver circulation thesis. The route's continuation into the Bourbon free trade reforms (entry ex6, 1778) which eventually undermines its monopoly status: documented in the 1778 decree's text, which explicitly addresses Philippine trade.

entries.label: Cadiz -- the cosmopolitan gateway of the Spanish empire
entries.hint: From 1717 the Casa de Contratacion (the institution regulating American trade) relocates from Sevilla to Cadiz, making it the legal monopoly port for all colonial trade until the Decreto de Libre Comercio (1778, entry ex6) opens multiple ports; Cadiz becomes one of Europe's most cosmopolitan cities -- merchant colonies from Genoa, France, Britain, Ireland and the Hanseatic cities settle permanently, and the city's population triples during the century, making it briefly one of the richest cities in Europe.
entries.note: Casa de Contratacion relocation 1717: AGI (Archivo General de Indias), which itself moved to Sevilla later (1785) but Cadiz remained the operational port. Foreign merchant colonies: documented in Cadiz municipal records and consular archives (British, Irish, French, Genoese consulates all maintained Cadiz offices). Garcia-Baquero Gonzalez (1976), Cadiz y el Atlantico 1717-1778: the standard study, using AGI trade statistics. The population growth: from c.40,000 (1700) to over 70,000 (1786) -- documented in census records.

entries.label: Family Compact — Bourbon alliance with France
entries.hint: Three Family Compacts between French and Spanish Bourbon branches (1733, 1743, 1761) coordinate policy against Britain — the Fourth Compact (Treaty of Aranjuez 1779) formally brings Spain into the American Revolutionary War; together they draw Spain into the Seven Years War, the American war and eventually Napoleon's orbit.
entries.note: First Family Compact 1733: AGS Estado; Archives des Affaires Étrangères, Paris. Third Family Compact 1761: AGS Estado. Spain's role in the American War of Independence 1779–1783: documented in AGS Marina and AGS Guerra. Dull (2005), The French Navy and the Seven Years' War: analyses the Family Compact's strategic consequences.

entries.label: Treaty of Madrid (1750) -- Spain buys out the British asiento
entries.hint: By the Treaty of Madrid (5 October 1750), Spain pays Great Britain £100,000 to permanently abolish the asiento (the British monopoly contract to supply enslaved Africans to Spanish America, granted at Utrecht in 1713) and the navio de permiso (the annual British trade ship to the Indies); negotiated by Carvajal y Lancaster, this ends decades of legal British commercial intervention and smuggling in Spanish colonial ports and allows Spain to reassert exclusive maritime control over its empire -- a major mid-century reversal of the Utrecht settlement.
entries.note: Treaty of Madrid 5 Oct 1750: AHN Estado, British National Archives SP 94 -- the £100,000 payment documented in both archives. Carvajal y Lancaster's negotiation (entry k7): his correspondence AHN Estado. The connection to Utrecht's asiento clause (entry c7): direct reversal, documented. Kamen (2001), Philip V of Spain, ch.10: contextualises the asiento's role in Anglo-Spanish relations 1713-1750. The treaty's role in reducing British smuggling: documented in subsequent customs records (AGI).

entries.label: Decreto de Libre Comercio — opening the colonial trade
entries.hint: Charles III ends the Cádiz monopoly on colonial trade — his Free Trade Decree allows 13 Spanish ports to trade directly with the Americas; colonial trade increases 700% between 1778 and 1796, the most dramatic economic expansion in Spanish colonial history.
entries.note: Decreto de Libre Comercio 12 Oct 1778: Gaceta de Madrid. AGI, Indiferente General: trade statistics before and after. The 700% growth figure: Fisher (1985), Commercial Relations between Spain and Spanish America in the Era of Free Trade. Lynch (1989), Bourbon Spain, ch.7. The decree's text is explicit about ending the Cádiz monopoly. The Catalan textile renaissance triggered by the decree: documented in the records of the Barcelona Chamber of Commerce and in Vilar (1962), La Catalogne dans l'Espagne moderne — Catalan cotton exports to the Americas increase dramatically from 1778. The decree's selective benefit: Catalan manufacturers gain most; Castilian artisans lose the captive colonial market.

entries.label: Treaty of Aranjuez — the Fourth Family Compact
entries.hint: The Treaty of Aranjuez (1779) is the Fourth Family Compact — Spain joins France against Britain in the American Revolutionary War; Spain's war goals focus on recovering Gibraltar; the siege of Gibraltar (1779–1783) becomes the longest siege in British military history.
entries.note: Treaty of Aranjuez 12 Apr 1779: AGS Estado and Archives des Affaires Étrangères. The siege of Gibraltar 1779–1783: British National Archives (WO series) and AGS Marina. Dull (1985), A Diplomatic History of the American Revolution: analyses the treaty. Minorca recovered (1782): Treaty of Paris 1783. Gibraltar not recovered: the fundamental Spanish strategic failure documented.

entries.label: Treaty of San Ildefonso and the War of the Oranges
entries.hint: Godoy signs the Treaty of San Ildefonso (1796) — an offensive alliance with Revolutionary France leading directly to the War of the Oranges (Spain invades Portugal 1801 at Napoleon's behest) and to Trafalgar (1805); the treaty is the mechanism of Spain's fatal entanglement with Napoleon.
entries.note: Treaty of San Ildefonso 18 Aug 1796: AGS Estado. The War of the Oranges May–Jun 1801: AGS Guerra. La Parra López (1992), La alianza de Godoy con los revolucionarios: the standard study. The consequential chain: San Ildefonso (1796) → Trafalgar (1805) → Bayonne (1808) — documented in AGS and Archives des Affaires Étrangères.

entries.label: Treaty of Fontainebleau (1807) — the Trojan Horse
entries.hint: Godoy signs the Treaty of Fontainebleau allowing French troops to cross Spain to invade Portugal — Napoleon uses this military access as a Trojan Horse; he occupies key Spanish fortresses (Pamplona, Barcelona, San Sebastián), refuses to withdraw, deposes the Bourbon dynasty at Bayonne (May 1808), and installs his brother Joseph; the Peninsular War begins.
entries.note: Treaty of Fontainebleau 27 Oct 1807: AGS Estado legajo 8174 — the primary document. The fortress occupations: documented in Suchet's memoirs and French military records. La Parra (2002), Manuel Godoy: the standard biography — analyses Godoy's miscalculation. The connection to Bayonne: the French troop deployment facilitated by Fontainebleau makes the May 1808 coup possible — documented. This is the single most consequential treaty in Spanish history for the 19th century.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: Bourbon Spain in its European and Atlantic context

entries.label: Bernardo de Galvez -- the Gulf Coast campaigns and Pensacola (1781)
entries.loc: Louisiana; West Florida (Mobile, Baton Rouge, Pensacola)
entries.hint: Bernardo de Galvez, Governor of Spanish Louisiana, leads a series of brilliant campaigns along the Gulf Coast during the American Revolutionary War -- capturing Baton Rouge and Mobile (1779-1780) and culminating in the two-month siege and Battle of Pensacola (Mar-May 1781), which recaptures West Florida from Britain; his victories keep British forces permanently divided between fronts and secure the southern supply route for the American revolutionaries -- Spain's single most significant geopolitical contribution to American independence.
entries.note: Battle of Pensacola (9 Mar-8 May 1781): AGS Guerra -- Galvez's own dispatches. The capture of Baton Rouge (Sep 1779) and Mobile (Mar 1780): documented in the same series. Caughey (1934), Bernardo de Galvez in Louisiana 1776-1783: the standard English study. The US Congress's 2014 honorary citizenship for Galvez: documented as modern recognition of this campaign's significance. The connection to Gibraltar (entry c9) and the Treaty of Aranjuez (entry ex3): all part of Spain's coordinated 1779-1783 war effort.

entries.label: American independence movements — the empire's beginning of the end
entries.hint: The Bourbon reforms' success in enriching creole elites, combined with the example of the American and French revolutions, begins the process of independence — Tupac Amaru II's revolt in Peru (1780) is the largest colonial uprising before independence; Miranda's conspiracies begin in the 1790s.
entries.note: Tupac Amaru II revolt 1780–1781: documented in AGI Lima — the most detailed colonial uprising record. Francisco de Miranda's correspondence: Archivo del General Miranda (Caracas) and British Foreign Office records (Kew). Lynch (1973), The Spanish American Revolutions: the standard study. The American Declaration of Independence's Spanish translation: circulated in Latin America, documented in colonial records.

entries.label: Nootka Crisis (1789-1790) -- Spain's Pacific sovereignty claim collapses
entries.hint: Spanish seizure of British vessels at Nootka Sound (1789), asserting Spain's traditional claim to exclusive sovereignty over the entire Pacific coast of the Americas, nearly triggers war with Britain; facing British naval superiority and without French support (the Revolution having paralysed the Bourbon Family Compact), Spain backs down -- the Nootka Convention (1790) abandons exclusive sovereignty claims and opens the Pacific Northwest to British and other powers, marking the definitive end of Spain's centuries-old claim to a closed Pacific.
entries.note: Nootka Convention 28 Oct 1790: AHN Estado, British National Archives FO 72. The crisis and Spain's diplomatic isolation (no French support post-1789): documented in Floridablanca's correspondence (entry k6) -- a direct consequence of the Revolution disrupting the Family Compact (entry ex1). Cook (1973), Flood Tide of Empire: Spain and the Pacific Northwest 1543-1819: the standard English study. The Convention's significance: the last major assertion and the first major renunciation of Spain's mare clausum (closed sea) doctrine in the Pacific.

entries.label: Fontainebleau geopolitical trap — Spain's fatal alliance with Napoleon
entries.hint: Spain's late Bourbon period ends in a geopolitical trap — the San Ildefonso alliance (1796), Trafalgar (1805), Fontainebleau (1807) and Bayonne (1808) form a documented causal chain; Spain enters as France's junior ally against Britain and ends as France's occupied territory; the same Napoleon whose alliance Godoy sought becomes Spain's conqueror.
entries.note: The causal chain: San Ildefonso (1796) → Trafalgar (1805) → Fontainebleau (1807) → Bayonne abdications (1808) — each step documented in AGS Estado and Archives des Affaires Étrangères. La Parra López (1992), La alianza de Godoy con los revolucionarios: the standard study. The Bayonne abdications (May 1808): Joseph Buonaparte's appointment documented in Gaceta de Madrid. This entry synthesises the Exchange and conflict entries into their World-level consequence.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored

entries.label: Coartacion -- the Spanish colonial path to self-purchased freedom
entries.hint: Spanish colonial law grants enslaved people the right of coartacion -- to have their market value officially appraised by a notary and then purchase their freedom in instalments, paying down their own price over years; enslaved people under coartacion (coartados) could also change owners if mistreated, by finding a buyer willing to pay the appraised price; this distinct legal mechanism -- absent from British and French Caribbean chattel systems -- produces, by 1800, one of the largest free Black populations (libres de color) in the Americas, concentrated in Havana.
entries.note: Coartacion notarial records: Archivo Nacional de Cuba, Escribanias -- thousands of individual appraisal and payment-plan documents survive. The Codigo Negro Carolino (1789, Cuba): AGI, the attempted codification of slave law including coartacion. de la Fuente (2007), 'Slave Law and Claims-Making in Cuba', Law and History Review: the standard modern study using the notarial archive. The libres de color population of Havana (c.20,000 by 1800, roughly a quarter of the city): documented in colonial census records (AGI, Cuba). This is the institutional precondition for the free Black communities documented across Cuba's later history (cross-reference: slavery's abolition in Cuba, 1886, in the 1808-1900 folio).

entries.label: Gran Redada de Gitanos — 12,000 Roma arrested in a single night
entries.hint: On the night of 30 July 1749, the Marquess of Ensenada orchestrates the simultaneous arrest of approximately 12,000 Roma (gitanos) across Spain — men and boys over seven sent to forced labour in royal naval arsenals (Cartagena, El Ferrol, Cádiz); women and children imprisoned in factories and castles; an attempt at cultural and biological eradication of a community.
entries.note: The Gran Redada planning documents: AGS, Marina legajos 301–303 and Gracia y Justicia legajos — the operation's logistics documented in extraordinary detail. The prisoner count (approximately 12,000): documented in AGS registers. The arsenal labour records: AGS Marina. The women's imprisonment at Santa Bárbara castle (Palencia): documented. Leblon (1985), Les Gitans d'Espagne: the standard study. The operation's failure: by 1763 the government had released most prisoners — the eradication attempt collapsed. This is the most severe documented state action against a community in 18th-century Spain.

entries.label: Cigarreras of Seville -- a female industrial workforce a century early
entries.hint: The Royal Tobacco Factory in Seville -- the largest industrial building in Spain when completed in 1758 -- employs thousands of women, the cigarreras, as cigar rollers; far from marginalised, they were a relatively well-paid, socially cohesive labour elite with their own mutual-aid associations and a proud public identity -- among the first financially independent women in Spain, nearly a century before industrialisation reached the rest of the country. Their reputation, later mythologised in Bizet's Carmen (1875), has documented roots in this 18th-century workplace pride.
entries.note: Real Fabrica de Tabacos de Sevilla: completed 1758, extant building (now University of Seville rectorate). Factory administrative records: Archivo General de Indias, Real Hacienda -- tobacco was a royal monopoly administered alongside colonial trade. Rodriguez Gordillo (1984), La creacion del estanco del tabaco en Espana: the standard study of the monopoly and its workforce. Cigarreras' wages relative to other female employment: documented as notably higher -- AGI payroll records. Merimee's 1845 novella (source for Bizet's opera) explicitly draws on this established occupational identity.

entries.label: Majos y majas -- popular culture as class performance
entries.hint: The majos and majas -- working-class Madrid men and women who cultivated an exaggerated, flamboyant Spanish dress and manner (capes, sashes, elaborate hair nets) -- emerge as a distinct urban subculture partly in deliberate opposition to the French-influenced fashions (afrancesados) of the court and aristocracy; their style is adopted ironically by aristocratic women (majas de rango) as a fashionable affectation, while for working Madrilenos it remains a genuine assertion of popular identity; Goya's portraits (including the Majas, 1797-1800) document both registers.
entries.note: Goya's La maja vestida and La maja desnuda (1797-1800, Museo del Prado): the primary visual documents -- both registers (genuine popular dress vs aristocratic appropriation) are documented in contemporary satirical literature (sainetes by Ramon de la Cruz, BNE). The afrancesado/majo opposition: documented in police and municipal dress-code records (Madrid municipal archive). Carmona Garcia (1991), Pueblo y nobleza: the manolos y madamitas: the standard study of this class-performance dynamic. The connection to the Esquilache riots (c3, 1766, partly triggered by a ban on traditional capes and wide-brimmed hats): direct documented link.

entries.label: Maria Isidra de Guzman -- the first woman doctorate in Spain (1785)
entries.hint: Maria Isidra de Guzman y de la Cerda receives a doctorate in Philosophy and Letters from the Universidad de Alcala de Henares on 7 June 1785 -- examined publicly before the full university and the court -- becoming the first woman in Spain to hold a doctoral degree; Charles III personally attends the ceremony and grants her the unprecedented honorific title of 'doctora honoraria' of the Real Academia Espanola, though she is never permitted to teach or practise; her case is celebrated by Amar y Borbon (entry lv1) as proof of women's intellectual capacity.
entries.note: Doctoral examination 7 Jun 1785: Universidad de Alcala archive (now Universidad Complutense, Archivo Historico) -- the formal record survives. Charles III's attendance and the RAE honorary title: documented in contemporary press (Gaceta de Madrid, Jun 1785, BNE). The contrast with her never being permitted to teach: documented -- the honour was symbolic, not professional. Lopez-Cordon (2005), 'Mujer y poder en la Espana del siglo XVIII': contextualises her case alongside Amar y Borbon's contemporaneous feminist writing.

entries.label: Josefa Amar y Borbón — Spanish Enlightenment feminism
entries.hint: Josefa Amar y Borbón publishes the Discurso en defensa del talento de las mujeres (1786) and Discurso sobre la educación física y moral de las mujeres (1790) — the most substantial Spanish Enlightenment argument for women's equal education; she is admitted to the Aragonese Economic Society over male objections.
entries.note: Discurso en defensa del talento de las mujeres (1786): first published in Memorial Literario (Madrid) — BNE. Discurso sobre la educación (1790): BNE first edition. Her admission to the Aragonese Economic Society: documented in the society's minutes (Archivo de la RSEAP de Aragón). Bolufer Peruga (1998), Mujeres e Ilustración: the standard study of women in the Spanish Enlightenment.

entries.label: The Bourbon Inquisition and sodomy — declining but not abolished
entries.hint: Sodomy jurisdiction in Castile had always resided primarily with secular courts under the Catholic Monarchs' pragmática (1497); in the Crown of Aragon, the Inquisition held specific jurisdiction since a 1524 papal brief; Bourbon reformers curtail this Aragonese Inquisition jurisdiction across the 18th century as part of broader efforts to limit Church courts — but no criminal code formalising this was ever enacted; Spain's first Penal Code is 1822.
entries.note: The Catholic Monarchs' pragmática on sodomy (1497): documented — assigns jurisdiction to secular courts in Castile. The 1524 papal brief for the Aragonese Inquisition: documented in AHN Inquisición. The Bourbon restriction of Inquisition jurisdiction: documented in AHN Inquisición series — declining caseloads. Spain's first Penal Code: 1822 (Liberal Triennium). Lardizábal's unenacted reform draft c.1787: AHN Consejos — never approved. Berco (2007), Sexual Hierarchies: the standard study of sodomy prosecution across both periods.

events.l: War of Jenkins' Ear / Austrian Succession
Napoleonic Wars -- Spain as French ally
Peace of Utrecht -- Spain's European losses
War of Jenkins' Ear -- Cartagena de Indias
War of Austrian Succession -- Italian theatre
War of the Pyrenees -- First Coalition
Cortes of Madrid -- secret Salic Law repeal
Inquisition under Bourbon reform -- declining authority
Moratin: El si de las ninas
Canal de Castilla -- internal market integration
Banco de San Carlos -- Spain's first national bank
Balmis Vaccine Expedition -- first global vaccination
Decreto de Libre Comercio -- colonial ports opened
Treaty of Aranjuez — Fourth Family Compact
San Ildefonso — Spain in Napoleon's orbit
Treaty of Fontainebleau — Napoleon's Trojan Horse
Geopolitical trap — Spain from ally to occupied
Gran Redada — 12,000 Roma arrested in one night
Cigarreras of Seville -- female industrial workforce
Majos y majas -- popular culture as performance
Maria Isidra de Guzman -- first doctorate
